title: Python lists: keeping the most recent readings
learning_outcome: Use a Python list to store, append, and remove values so that only the most recent fixed number of readings is retained.
challenge_prompt: A sensor produces one new heart-rate reading at a time. Without using a full explanation of lists yet, describe how you would keep only the 10 most recent readings and discard older readings as new readings arrive.
expected_reasoning_steps: Represent multiple readings as one ordered collection.
Add each new reading to the end of the collection.
Check whether the collection contains more than 10 readings.
Remove the oldest reading when the collection grows beyond 10.
Use the updated collection as the current state.
common_misconceptions: Storing each reading in a separate variable instead of one collection.
Replacing the whole collection with the newest reading.
Removing the newest reading instead of the oldest reading.
Checking the value of a reading instead of checking the number of readings.
canonical_explanation: A Python list is an ordered collection that can grow and shrink. For this task, the useful structure is a sliding window: append the newest reading, then if the list is longer than 10, remove the first item because it is the oldest reading.
canonical_answer: Keep the readings in one list. For each new reading, append it to the end. If the list length becomes greater than 10, remove the item at index 0 because it is the oldest reading. The list then always contains the 10 most recent readings.
worked_example: Keep readings in one list. Append the newest reading. If the list length becomes 11, remove index 0 so only 10 remain.

source.title: Sample Programming Notes: Python Lists and Sliding Windows
source.excerpt: A list stores ordered values under one name. You can append a new value to the end of a list and remove an existing value by position. A sliding window keeps only the newest fixed number of items: append the newest item, then remove the oldest item when the list becomes too long.

rubric_items.feedback_if_missing: Use one ordered collection instead of ten separate variables or a string.

rubric_items.criterion: Adds each new reading to the end
rubric_items.feedback_if_missing: State how each new reading enters the collection, ideally by appending it.

rubric_items.criterion: Checks whether more than 10 readings are stored
rubric_items.feedback_if_missing: Check the number of stored readings after adding the newest reading.

rubric_items.criterion: Removes the oldest reading when over limit
rubric_items.feedback_if_missing: Remove the first/oldest item, not the newest item.

retrieval_questions.question_text: Why is a list more suitable than ten separate variables for this task?
retrieval_questions.expected_answer: A list stores an ordered collection under one name and can grow or shrink as readings arrive.
retrieval_questions.scoring_guidance: Look for ordered collection and easier update logic.

retrieval_questions.question_text: What should happen immediately after the eleventh reading is added?
retrieval_questions.expected_answer: The oldest reading should be removed so the list returns to 10 readings.
retrieval_questions.scoring_guidance: The answer must remove the oldest item, not the newest item.

retrieval_questions.question_text: Transfer: How would the logic change if the system needed the most recent seven readings?
retrieval_questions.expected_answer: Append the new reading, then remove the oldest reading when the list length is greater than seven.
retrieval_questions.scoring_guidance: The answer should transfer the sliding-window rule from 10 to 7.
